Through the day on February 20, 2018 numerous accidents occurred as a result of widespread accumulating ice. Patchy freezing drizzle started as early as midnight on the morning of February 20, but the heavier rain fell through the morning and early afternoon hours. Numerous powerlines and trees sustained some damage from the accumulation of 1/8 to 1/3 inch of ice across a widespread area, including the Kansas City metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Through the day on February 20, 2018 numerous accidents occurred as a result of widespread accumulating ice. Patchy freezing drizzle started as early as midnight on the morning of February 20, but the heavier rain fell through the morning and early afternoon hours. Numerous powerlines and trees sustained some damage from the accumulation of 1/8 to 1/3 inch of ice across a widespread area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a quasi-stationary front that was draped across the area. One long-lived supercell produced a brief tornado northwest of Ark City before exiting into Oklahoma and becoming stationary. The supercell then moved north and produced multiple tornadoes as a large bow echo thunderstorm merged with the supercell in central Cowley County. This was quickly followed by several shorter-lived Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) tornadoes north and east of Winfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very brief touchdown in open country.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-deck wave pushed a strong cold front across Kansas throughout the night and early morning of the 19th and 20th. The result was that widespread sleet and freezing rain occurred across all of the WFO Wichita County Warning Area with most of the adverse winter weather occurring on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 0.25 inch of glaze accumulated on trees and power lines 5 miles north of Altoona. Road surfaces were beginning to freeze.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-deck wave pushed a strong cold front across Kansas throughout the night and early morning of the 19th and 20th. The result was that widespread sleet and freezing rain occurred across all of the WFO Wichita County Warning Area with most of the adverse winter weather occurring on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced around 0.25 inch of glaze on trees and power lines 5 miles east southeast of Humboldt and around 0.12 inch 2 miles east of Iola. In several areas, the power lines began to sag. Glazing did not occur on roads and highways.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain downed a few limbs in Coffeyville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Torrential rains caused significant rises on the Saline River. The river spilled out of its banks in and around Fairport, where water 2 to 4 feet deep inundated a small section of Fairport Road near the bridges. Sand bags were placed around one home. Floods also occurred across a section of this road about 1 mile southeast of Fairport on 176th Street.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The very heavy rains caused such significant rises on the Saline River that it overflowed around Fairport. Fairport Road was flooded at both the east and west entrances into Fairport. One section of Fairport Road was flooded 2 to 4 feet deep near the river bridges. Sand bags were placed around 1 home. Flooding also occurred about 1 mile southeast of Fairport on 176th Street.

The supercell then moved north and produced multiple tornadoes as a large bow echo thunderstorm merged with the supercell in central Cowley County. This was quickly followed by several shorter-lived Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) tornadoes north and east of Winfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning through mid afternoon freezing drizzle occurred over Northwest Kansas.  Ice accumulations ranged from a trace up to 0.38, with the highest ice accumulation reported in St. Francis.  Mid afternoon a band of moderate to heavy snow moved east into Kansas.  During the night the first band gradually ended as a second band of moderate to heavy snow developed and moved east.  Wind gusts were mostly in the 20-40 MPH range, but the strongest wind gust reported was 57 MPH at Goodland.  The combination of moderate to heavy snow along with strong winds caused zero to near zero visibility under each band of snow.  Highway 40 and 83 were closed in Logan County due to the hazardous driving conditions, stranding many motorists in Oakley.  Atleast one motorist was stranded on Highway 40.  The highest snowfall amount was 13 inches reported in northwest Sherman County.  Snowfall amounts declined to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall across the county was around 11 inches.  Duration of the wind gusts during the storm were below blizzard criteria.  However blizzard like conditions were observed.  Prior to the snow occurring freezing drizzle fell, with ice accumulation totals of 0.25 to 0.38.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The anomalously dry period that plagued the region during the summer of 2018 continued into and through July. Most areas were about 2 inches short of normal precipitation for the month of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The abnormally dry summer continued into and through July for Johnson County. The Drought Monitor put the county in D3 and maintained it into August. As of yet, the breadth and magnitude of the impacts are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low moved out of the Four Corners region and into the Southern Rockies before ejecting northeastward across Kansas. Very strong north winds were observed behind the associated cold front that swept through late on the 10th and into the 11th. Some of the precipitation associated with the upper level system turned to snow.  There was at least 3 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed at the Garden City airport at 1134 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation picked up during August, especially in some of the hardest hit drought areas, but in a lot of cases the damage had already been done, and while the rains did pick back up the ground soil was so parched that it made hardly a dent in the drought across northeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While rain did pick up across the area through the month of August, drought conditions saw some improvement by the end of the month. The impacts and economic impact across far eastern Kansas as a result of the drought are as of yet unclear, but across much of the Midwest several crops and fields were deemed unfit for harvest and were either lost or baled for cattle feed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of January 11th, 2018, an area of low pressure rapidly moved northeast from the Texas Panhandle. As the low pressure area moved northeast, extremely cold temperatures to the northwest of the low plunged south across portions of central and south central Kansas.  This caused the precipitation to rapidly turn over to freezing rain, sleet, and eventually snow. This quick turnover rapidly made roadways very slick, with numerous accidents and slide offs associated with the slippery roadways.  The heaviest snowfall occurred across Northwest Saline County where 4 to 5 inch accumulations were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of freezing rain, sleet and snow led to treacherous driving conditions across most of Saline County, Kansas.  Numerous accidents and slide offs were reported along Interstate 70 and Interstate 135 near Salina.  The heaviest snowfall occurred to the northwest of Salina where 4 to 5 inches were reported.

As the low pressure area moved northeast, extremely cold temperatures to the northwest of the low plunged south across portions of central and south central Kansas.  This caused the precipitation to rapidly turn over to freezing rain, sleet, and eventually snow. This quick turnover rapidly made roadways very slick, with numerous accidents and slide offs associated with the slippery roadways.  The heaviest snowfall occurred across Northwest Saline County where 4 to 5 inch accumulations were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of freezing rain, sleet and snow spread across McPherson County, Kansas early in the morning of January 11th, 2018. This made travel dangerous as numerous accidents and slide-offs occurred. The greatest snow accumulations, 2 to 3 inches, were reported across Western and Northern McPherson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of one-quarter mile visibility occurred along with frequent wind gusts between 40 and 50 mph on this Monday. Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with Plainville reporting the highest amount of 6 inches. Webster Dam and Logan measured 4 inches with 3 inches in Phillipsburg. This snow storm was associated with low pressure that moved east across Kansas and deepened near Kansas City during the morning hours. This low then occluded over Iowa. This system formed as an upper-level trough over the western U.S. moved east and intensified over Colorado.

Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with Plainville reporting the highest amount of 6 inches. Webster Dam and Logan measured 4 inches with 3 inches in Phillipsburg. An intense cyclone that moved northeast across Kansas enabled northwest winds from 30 to 40 mph to lash the region. Gusts reached 40 to 50 mph and were certainly strong enough to cause some tree damage in parts of South-Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CO-OP observer in Bluff City reported that the strong winds downed a 6-inch diameter limb in their back yard. Since the tree damage was not discovered until the following morning, the time of the event is an estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning through mid afternoon freezing drizzle occurred over Northwest Kansas.  Ice accumulations ranged from a trace up to 0.38, with the highest ice accumulation reported in St. Francis.  Mid afternoon a band of moderate to heavy snow moved east into Kansas.  During the night the first band gradually ended as a second band of moderate to heavy snow developed and moved east.  Wind gusts were mostly in the 20-40 MPH range, but the strongest wind gust reported was 57 MPH at Goodland.  The combination of moderate to heavy snow along with strong winds caused zero to near zero visibility under each band of snow.  Highway 40 and 83 were closed in Logan County due to the hazardous driving conditions, stranding many motorists in Oakley.  Atleast one motorist was stranded on Highway 40.  The highest snowfall amount was 13 inches reported in northwest Sherman County.  Snowfall amounts declined to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall across the county ranged from 6 to 13 inches from south to north.  Prior to the snow occurring 0.10 of ice accumulation was reported in Goodland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread high winds occurred on this Monday and Tuesday. Between midnight and 6 AM CST Monday, a triple point low and associated fronts swept across north central Kansas. During the day Monday, several lows consolidated into a primary low along the South Dakota-Minnesota border. This low became nearly stationary as the fronts continued to progress far to the east and south. Monday night this low performed a loop and moved southeast into Iowa. During the day Tuesday, this low rapidly filled and became absorbed into a low over the Great Lakes. Strong winds occurred from the late morning into the afternoon hours Monday and Tuesday due to diurnal mixing. Widespread wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph occurred on both days with the highest wind gust occurring at Logan. The high winds combined with dry fuels and relative humidity levels as low as 23% to create favorable conditions for the wildfire that occurred near Kensington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a wildfire approximately 7 miles northeast of Kensington in Smith County during the mid-late afternoon hours, located near the intersection of F Road and 100 Road. This fire burned between 2500-3000 acres, and did burn some abandoned structures. There were 5 fire departments on the scene. While the fire was under control late in the afternoon, a few firefighters lingered into the evening hours to keep an eye on the situation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level system moved across a moderately unstable atmosphere and produced widespread snow across much of the area.  The heaviest amounts were north of highway 50 and along and west of highway 283.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 2 to 4 inches was common across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although not a major storm by any means, this Sunday morning featured a quick-hitting burst of fluffy, wind-driven snow that roughly doubled accumulation expectations across portions of North Central Kansas, especially within Jewell and Smith counties where 2-4 was common.  According to reports from official NWS cooperative observers, some of the highest totals in the local six-county area featured: 4.0 at Burr Oak, 3.0 at Jewell and Lebanon, and 2.6 at Smith Center. Although even these totals were relatively modest by Central Plains standards, this event packed somewhat of a punch given sustained north winds generally 20-25 MPH/gusts 30+ MPH, resulting in some drifting and at least brief visibility reductions to one-half mile or less. ||As for timing, the vast majority of accumulating snow fell between 5AM-Noon, gradually ending from north-to-south. Forcing-wise, this event was driven by a classic case of pronounced mid-level frontogenesis, trailing well-behind a strong surface cold front that passed through during the preceding 24 hours. On the larger scale, a broad shortwave trough tracked southeast from the Northern Rockies/Dakotas into the Midwest over the course of Feb. 4th. Locally, this snow event was accompanied by a very cold airmass featuring surface temperatures mainly in the single digits to around 10 degrees F, and wind chill values ranging from -5 to -15 F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total 4.0 inches was measured in Burr Oak, and also 3.0 inches fell in Jewell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first severe thunderstorms of the season occurred over Phillips and Smith counties on this Friday evening. A large area of showers evolved over southwest Nebraska. Around 6 PM CST, a thunderstorm formed at the southern edge of these showers along the Nebraska-Kansas state line, south of McCook. Over the next two hours, a small cyclonic-shaped arc of thunderstorms formed straddling the state line. The southernmost part of this arc moved east across Phillips and Smith counties between 7 and 9 PM CST. Severe hail occurred along most of the storms path from Logan to just south of Phillipsburg to Smith Center and Lebanon. Most of the hail reported was around 1 inch in diameter, but hail the size of ping pong balls was reported in Smith Center and golf ball size hail was reported near Glade. This storm produced lots of hail. One observer reported the ground was nearly covered in Smith Center. Severe winds also occurred over parts of Phillips county where winds were measured as high as 66 mph in Logan.||Cyclogenesis was occurring over eastern Colorado during the morning hours. As the low moved into western Kansas in the afternoon, a front that had been stationary from northwest to southeast across Kansas began moving north. By the time the storms formed, the front had moved to the Nebraska-Kansas border. These storms developed at the apex of the warm sector, near surface low, where temperatures were in the upper 70s and dewpoints were near 50. The cyclonic arc shape was to due the storms forming within a ribbon of mid-level frontogenesis. An elevated mixed layer had overspread the area, but the meager surface dewpoints resulted in weak instability with surface-based CAPE near 1000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was around 60 kt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging in size from pennies to golf balls was reported along this path.

According to reports from official NWS cooperative observers, some of the highest totals featured: 4��� in Logan, 3.2��� at Smith Center and Lebanon, and 3��� at Kirwin Dam. ||As for timing, most of the area received the majority of snow between 3-10 PM. However, it started accumulating as early as 1 PM in the Phillips County area, and lingered until around midnight in and near Mitchell County. Aloft, a large-scale, broad trough was translating through the central United States along with a series of upper jet streaks. This setup yielded a concentrated swath of mid-level frontogenesis, which in turn promoted a fairly narrow, west-east oriented snow band that dropped the majority of accumulation. At the surface, this was certainly a ���cold snow��� with temperatures only averaging around 10 F. The relatively light winds were promoted by a nearby high pressure center in northeast Nebraska (roughly 1026 millibars).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 2.0 inches occurred in Covert.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of severe thunderstorms and a few supercells moved east across central Kansas | and along Interstate 70, during the late afternoon and evening hours of May 1st, 2018.  The strongest supercell thunderstorms produced very large hail up to 4 inches in diameter.  A couple of tornadoes were produced by one strong supercell thunderstorm, the first touched down just south of Interstate 70, seven miles north of Ellsworth, and the second touched down,  3 miles north of Glendale, or 7 miles northwest of Salina, Kansas.  Damage from the tornadoes was minimal, however, significant damage occurred due to the very large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The report came from National Severe Storms Lab mobile mesonet team while out chasing.

A few tornadoes were also reported along the leading edge of the quasi linear convective system with only brief touchdowns reported.

During the late morning and through the early afternoon high winds on the backside of an upper level storm system reached the ground.  This resulted in wind gusts of 60 to 70 MPH.  The peak wind gust occurred in Hoxie.  The high winds also caused damage, including blowing over three semis pulling empty trailers, breaking off a five inch diameter tree limb, and blowing over a communications tower.  ||The high winds also created a dust storm at many locations leading to zero visibility.  The pockets of blowing dust originated near a weak stationary front draped across Northwest Kansas, then slowly moved south with the weak front through the afternoon.

Severe thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a quasi-stationary front that was draped across the area. One long-lived supercell produced a brief tornado northwest of Ark City before exiting into Oklahoma and becoming stationary. The supercell then moved north and produced multiple tornadoes as a large bow echo thunderstorm merged with the supercell in central Cowley County. This was quickly followed by several shorter-lived Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) tornadoes north and east of Winfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fifty to fifty five mph wind gusts were also reported with the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms move east across the southern sections of Kansas producing large hail, very strong winds, and heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report from social media. One half inch hail fell for about 3 or 4 minutes before changing to one inch hail or slightly larger for a brief time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first severe thunderstorms of the season occurred over Phillips and Smith counties on this Friday evening. A large area of showers evolved over southwest Nebraska. Around 6 PM CST, a thunderstorm formed at the southern edge of these showers along the Nebraska-Kansas state line, south of McCook. Over the next two hours, a small cyclonic-shaped arc of thunderstorms formed straddling the state line. The southernmost part of this arc moved east across Phillips and Smith counties between 7 and 9 PM CST. Severe hail occurred along most of the storms path from Logan to just south of Phillipsburg to Smith Center and Lebanon. Most of the hail reported was around 1 inch in diameter, but hail the size of ping pong balls was reported in Smith Center and golf ball size hail was reported near Glade. This storm produced lots of hail. One observer reported the ground was nearly covered in Smith Center. Severe winds also occurred over parts of Phillips county where winds were measured as high as 66 mph in Logan.||Cyclogenesis was occurring over eastern Colorado during the morning hours. As the low moved into western Kansas in the afternoon, a front that had been stationary from northwest to southeast across Kansas began moving north. By the time the storms formed, the front had moved to the Nebraska-Kansas border. These storms developed at the apex of the warm sector, near surface low, where temperatures were in the upper 70s and dewpoints were near 50. The cyclonic arc shape was to due the storms forming within a ribbon of mid-level frontogenesis. An elevated mixed layer had overspread the area, but the meager surface dewpoints resulted in weak instability with surface-based CAPE near 1000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was around 60 kt.

Severe to extreme drought persisted over a good portions of central and south central Kansas during the month of May.  Welcome rainfall during the beginning of the month and again at the end of the month, improved the drought some over south central Kansas by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions were over the entire county for early portion of the month, but welcome rainfall during the beginning and end of the month improved the drought conditions considerably by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms struck much of Central and South-Central Kansas with large hail and damaging winds. There were instances when & where the hail was very large, most notably on the south side of McPherson where it reached the size of baseballs. Gusts from 52 to 62 knots were measured in many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Windows were broken on several cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms struck much of Central and South-Central Kansas with large hail and damaging winds. There were instances when & where the hail was very large, most notably on the south side of McPherson where it reached the size of baseballs. Gusts from 52 to 62 knots were measured in many areas.

Numerous severe thunderstorms struck much of Central and South-Central Kansas with large hail and damaging winds. There were instances when & where the hail was very large, most notably on the south side of McPherson where it reached the size of baseballs. Gusts from 52 to 62 knots were measured in many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged from golf ball to baseball-sized. No damage was reported, but one would have to believe there was.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms swept east across most of the region during the afternoon and evening hours of May 2nd, 2018.  As the storms moved east, a combination of large hail and damaging winds moved across the area.  A few tornadoes were also reported along the leading edge of the quasi linear convective system with only brief touchdowns reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weak tornado formed on the leading edge of a bow echo and moved across Interstate 135. No damage was noted as it moved across an open field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread high winds occurred on this Monday and Tuesday. Between midnight and 6 AM CST Monday, a triple point low and associated fronts swept across north central Kansas. During the day Monday, several lows consolidated into a primary low along the South Dakota-Minnesota border. This low became nearly stationary as the fronts continued to progress far to the east and south. Monday night this low performed a loop and moved southeast into Iowa. During the day Tuesday, this low rapidly filled and became absorbed into a low over the Great Lakes. Strong winds occurred from the late morning into the afternoon hours Monday and Tuesday due to diurnal mixing. Widespread wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph occurred on both days with the highest wind gust occurring at Logan. The high winds combined with dry fuels and relative humidity levels as low as 23% to create favorable conditions for the wildfire that occurred near Kensington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by a mesonet located 1 mile west of Logan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of severe thunderstorms and a few supercells moved east across central Kansas | and along Interstate 70, during the late afternoon and evening hours of May 1st, 2018.  The strongest supercell thunderstorms produced very large hail up to 4 inches in diameter.  A couple of tornadoes were produced by one strong supercell thunderstorm, the first touched down just south of Interstate 70, seven miles north of Ellsworth, and the second touched down,  3 miles north of Glendale, or 7 miles northwest of Salina, Kansas.  Damage from the tornadoes was minimal, however, significant damage occurred due to the very large hail.

During the late morning and through the early afternoon high winds on the backside of an upper level storm system reached the ground.  This resulted in wind gusts of 60 to 70 MPH.  The peak wind gust occurred in Hoxie.  The high winds also caused damage, including blowing over three semis pulling empty trailers, breaking off a five inch diameter tree limb, and blowing over a communications tower.  ||The high winds also created a dust storm at many locations leading to zero visibility.  The pockets of blowing dust originated near a weak stationary front draped across Northwest Kansas, then slowly moved south with the weak front through the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zero visibility reported from Winona west to the county line and north from Winona to the county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cluster of t-storms developed during the afternoon of May 2 across central Kansas. The cluster evolved into a line or lines of storms that moved across northeast and east central Kansas during the afternoon and evening of May 2.  Wind damage reports occurred in addition to a few tornado reports with the lines.  At least 3 documented tornadoes occurred but all were short lived and relatively weak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report relayed by Sheriff of a brief tornado in open field did no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the latter half of the afternoon strong to severe thunderstorms moved north across Northwest Kansas.  Storm coverage increased in the late afternoon along an outflow boundary from one of storms as it moved north.  Behind the outflow boundary wind gusts around 60 MPH were reported, which caused some damage.   The largest hail reported was golf ball size near Colby.
A Quasi Linear Convective System starting on November 30th and ending on December 1st produced 60 to 70 mph winds that damaged outbuildings and downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds broke three power poles and overturned a 33ft RV. Time based on radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm produced a tornado, across northern portions of Saline county, Kansas, 7 miles northwest of Salina, Kansas.  When the tornado touched down, it produced damage to a metal barn. Part of the roof had been blown off and some of the side walls had been blown out. The tornado continued to track to the northwest and moved into Ottawa county, Kansas, just to the east of the town of Tescott.  The tornado was on the ground for 1.6 miles, in Saline county, and had a path width of 200 yards. A National Weather Service survey team, rated the tornado an EF1, across Saline county. Note: the tornado would go on to produce EF3 damage across Ottawa county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a quasi-stationary front that was draped across the area. One long-lived supercell produced a brief tornado northwest of Ark City before exiting into Oklahoma and becoming stationary. The supercell then moved north and produced multiple tornadoes as a large bow echo thunderstorm merged with the supercell in central Cowley County. This was quickly followed by several shorter-lived Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) tornadoes north and east of Winfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage was noted along the path.

A series of severe thunderstorms and a few supercells moved east across central Kansas | and along Interstate 70, during the late afternoon and evening hours of May 1st, 2018.  The strongest supercell thunderstorms produced very large hail up to 4 inches in diameter.  A couple of tornadoes were produced by one strong supercell thunderstorm, the first touched down just south of Interstate 70, seven miles north of Ellsworth, and the second touched down,  3 miles north of Glendale, or 7 miles northwest of Salina, Kansas.  Damage from the tornadoes was minimal, however, significant damage occurred due to the very large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sent via Twitter. The hail lasted for 10 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a quasi-stationary front that was draped across the area. One long-lived supercell produced a brief tornado northwest of Ark City before exiting into Oklahoma and becoming stationary. The supercell then moved north and produced multiple tornadoes as a large bow echo thunderstorm merged with the supercell in central Cowley County. This was quickly followed by several shorter-lived Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) tornadoes north and east of Winfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large electrical transmission lines were downed and a tree was completely uprooted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: KVOE radio relayed the report.

On the morning of March 26, 2018 scattered thunderstorms moved through eastern Kansas, producing marginally severe hail.

Behind a cold front, high winds on the backside of a weak upper level disturbance reached the ground in a few counties. In Sherman County, the peak gust was 61 MPH at Goodland. Wallace and Gove counties also experienced high winds.||To the east ahead of the cold front, a line of strong to severe thunderstorms was moving east across Decatur, Norton, and Graham counties.  These storms produced large hail and severe wind gusts.  The largest hail size reported was golf ball at Norton, and the highest wind gust was 63 MPH at Hill City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous ping-pong to golf ball size hail fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure center pushed well into Kansas overnight, and a surge of north winds developed in its wake. The pressure gradient tightened as a result of high pressure over the High Plains of western Nebraska and Kansas with 3-hr pressure rises on the order of 10-12 millibars. Behind the associated cold front, intense cold advection occurred from the surface through about 825mb, and this layer became well-mixed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind was observed at the Sublette airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds continued through much of the day once again as the surface to 500mb low moved across northern Kansas into northwest Missouri. Boundary layer mean winds across western Kansas ranged from 40 to 45 knots and 850mb winds were greater than 50 knots through much of Saturday. Wind gusts as high as 68 mph occurred through the mid-afternoon as sustained northwest winds howled at 30 to 40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The high wind gust was observed at a location 1 ENE of Garden City at 825 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong gusty northwest winds developed behind a cold front crossing western |Kansas through the morning as a deepening upper level low began to lift northeast out of the Central Rockies and into the West Central High Plains. Strong cold air advection behind this front developed during the afternoon as 50+ knot 850mb winds mixed down to the surface. Wind speeds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 55 to 60 became common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind was observed at the Syracuse airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One large wildfire moved in from Colorado and went through southwest Stanton county before turning south into Morton county.  Around 4000 acres were reported to be burned with several structures in both counties being lost to the fire.  Another fire burned south of Lakin in Kearny county but no structures were reported to be affected.  About 1500 acres were burned.

Ridging aloft transitioned eastward across the Central Plains giving way to an increasingly diffluent southwest flow aloft across the Western High Plains. Meanwhile, a prevailing southerly flow at the surface increased low level moisture across central and much of western Kansas. This increased instability with SBCAPE values pushing upward of 1000 J/kg ahead of a strengthening lee side trough in extreme eastern Colorado contributed to an unstable atmosphere. As steep low/mid level lapse rates developed by mid to late afternoon, a few isolated thunderstorms developed off a sharpening dryline near the Colorado border as H5 vort maxima lifted out of the Southern Rockies into the high plains. Weak flow aloft and marginal shear hindered severe potential.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ground was covered by the hail.

Wind gusts as high as 75 mph occurred throughout the day, causing significant blowing dust and wind damage across much of southwestern Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was observed at the Medicine Lodge airport at 12:02 AM CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Similar to the day before, an upper level shortwave trough lifted northeast across the Central Rockies resulting in an increasingly diffluent southwest flow aloft across the Western High Plains. Meanwhile, a prevailing southerly flow continued to draw  moisture into central and much of western Kansas ahead of an approaching dryline pushing surface dew points well up into the 50s(F). Increased instability with SBCAPE values upward of 1500 to 2000 J/kg and a modest +60kt upper level jet set the stage for a few isolated thunderstorms ahead of the dryline.

High winds during the morning and into the afternoon caused pockets of blowing dust to occur along a stationary front.  The GOES satellite detected the blowing dust.  However due to the sparce population, only one of the blowing dust pockets was confirmed.  The highest wind gust was 72 MPH measured near Weskan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak wind gust was measured near Weskan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a cold front, high winds on the backside of a weak upper level disturbance reached the ground in a few counties. In Sherman County, the peak gust was 61 MPH at Goodland. Wallace and Gove counties also experienced high winds.||To the east ahead of the cold front, a line of strong to severe thunderstorms was moving east across Decatur, Norton, and Graham counties.  These storms produced large hail and severe wind gusts.  The largest hail size reported was golf ball at Norton, and the highest wind gust was 63 MPH at Hill City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged from pea to quarter in size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high winds, blowing dust, and a wildfire to Northwest Kansas. The high winds led to some wind damage across the area. In addition, the winds caused widespread blowing dust with zero visibility, which resulted in at least 2 accidents on area roadways. Finally, as the winds brought down power lines in Weskan, a wildfire began and rapidly spread.  The fire was so large that fire departments from neighboring counties were asked to respond.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of dust storm with less than quarter mile visibility reported across Cheyenne County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high winds, blowing dust, and a wildfire to Northwest Kansas. The high winds led to some wind damage across the area. In addition, the winds caused widespread blowing dust with zero visibility, which resulted in at least 2 accidents on area roadways. Finally, as the winds brought down power lines in Weskan, a wildfire began and rapidly spread.  The fire was so large that fire departments from neighboring counties were asked to respond.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wildfire started just north of the town of Weskan. Cause was power lines snapped due to high winds with the cold front. Fire ran 7 miles to the southeast and reached approximately three quarters of a mile wide at times. Fire was detected by GOES-16 imagery as well as radar. At least two structures were lost along with numerous power poles. Damage to railroad and railroad bridge also noticed by NWS Employee passing through a few days later.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged from quarter to ping-pong ball size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system created blizzard conditions across part of Northwest Kansas.  The blizzard conditions caused some area highways to be closed.  Up to five inches of snow was reported with the winter storm system.  South of the snow, high winds were reported in some of the counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zero visibility reported along Highway 36 across the county.  Snowfall amounts ranged from three to five inches across the county.  Highway 27 was closed from Wheeler to the Nebraska border, and Highway 36 was closed from St. Francis west into Yuma County.

Locations generally north of Highway 400 saw most of the snow with some locations picking up around 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to four inches of wet snow fell across Chase county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered snow showers began moving east across far Northwest Kansas in the morning. There were several different waves of snow showers from the morning until after midnight. The repeated rounds of snow from these showers yielded snowfall amounts of atleast five inches. The highest snowfall amount of 5.3 was reported north of St. Francis. The highest snowfall amounts occurred over the northwest part of the county, with amounts declining to the southeast.  The icy roadway north of St. Francis impeded traffic for a brief time in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 13:15 local time seven semis were unable to climb a hill on Highway 27 near CR R.  This caused a total of twelve semis to be stopped on the highway at that location.  Local farmers had to tow the semis up the hill with tractors in order for traffic to flow again.  Five inches of snow had fallen when this occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high winds, blowing dust, and a wildfire to Northwest Kansas. The high winds led to some wind damage across the area. In addition, the winds caused widespread blowing dust with zero visibility, which resulted in at least 2 accidents on area roadways. Finally, as the winds brought down power lines in Weskan, a wildfire began and rapidly spread.  The fire was so large that fire departments from neighboring counties were asked to respond.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread 60 to 76 mph winds across Wallace County. Peak gust of 76 mph occurred 3 miles SE of Weskan at 4:24 pm MST.

A couple severe thunderstorms developed and moved across Northwest Kansas during the afternoon and evening.  The largest hailstone reported was hen egg size in Goodland.  The highest wind gust occurred at both Horace and Hill City of 58 MPH.  One was produced by a thunderstorm and the other from high winds above ground being directed to the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was reported at the Hill City airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system created blizzard conditions across part of Northwest Kansas.  The blizzard conditions caused some area highways to be closed.  Up to five inches of snow was reported with the winter storm system.  South of the snow, high winds were reported in some of the counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Salina Journal reported a teenage driver became stranded in the winter storm due to zero visibility.  The sheriff was able to rescue the teenager during the storm.  Based on radar and surface observations, blizzard conditions were intermittent across the county which did not allow for a consecutive three hours of blizzard conditions to occur.  Around three inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure center pushed well into Kansas overnight, and a surge of north winds developed in its wake. The pressure gradient tightened as a result of high pressure over the High Plains of western Nebraska and Kansas with 3-hr pressure rises on the order of 10-12 millibars. Behind the associated cold front, intense cold advection occurred from the surface through about 825mb, and this layer became well-mixed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind was observed at a location 1 ENE Garden City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds continued through much of the day once again as the surface to 500mb low moved across northern Kansas into northwest Missouri. Boundary layer mean winds across western Kansas ranged from 40 to 45 knots and 850mb winds were greater than 50 knots through much of Saturday. Wind gusts as high as 68 mph occurred through the mid-afternoon as sustained northwest winds howled at 30 to 40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The high wind gust was observed at a location 4 SSW Kismet at 935 AM.

A strong upper level shortwave moved from the northern Rockies into the Northern Plains. This helped push a cold front through the area with very strong northerly winds. Wind gusts as high as 75 mph occurred throughout the day, causing significant blowing dust and wind damage across much of southwestern Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pivot irrigation sprinkler was turned over by the high wind at a location 5 S Ingalls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening the group of thunderstorms from East Central Colorado moved into Northwest Kansas behind a cold front.  As the thunderstorms moved into Kansas they become an organized thunderstorm complex, turning into more of a wind threat than a hail threat.  Peak wind gusts from this event were estimated at 100 MPH from damage in Hoxie and in far southern Cheyenne County.  As this complex moved east, another area of prolonged thunderstorm activity behind the cold front was occurring over Graham County where several hours of heavy rainfall had occurred.  The many hours of prolonged rainfall added up to between four and six inches of rain for the northern half of Graham County, which lead to flash flooding in Hill City.  The largest hail size reported was golf ball at two different locations.

The largest hail size reported was golf ball at two different locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated wind gust of 85 MPH was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The flood waters moved into Hill City from the north, overwhelming the drainage tunnels under the town.  Water was deep enough in town to move into businesses. |Residents in the trailer park on the south side of town had to evacuate.  Two different families had to be water rescued due to their resistance to leave despite flood waters surrounding their home in both instances.  One trailer house was carried away by the flood waters.  A house in town, located near where the drainage tunnels empty, was engulfed with flood waters.  The city park and playground equipment, where the drainage for town empties, was almost completely submerged in water.  Over six inches of rainfall was measured in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early in the afternoon scattered thunderstorms developed north of a warm front.  Penny size hail was reported from one of these storms.  During the latter half of the afternoon a thunderstorm complex moved east out of Colorado into Northwest Kansas.  The complex continued to increase in coverage as it moved eastward, transitioning into more of a wind threat than a hail threat.  Winds from the thunderstorm complex blew trees down, some of them being large trees, blew power lines down, and damaged a grain bin.  There were very few hail reports with this thunderstorm complex, with the largest being only penny size from several locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon storm activity from East Central Colorado spread into Northwest Kansas.  The storm intensity increased as the storms moved into Kansas, but turned into more of a wind threat than a hail threat.  The peak wind gust reported was 77 MPH measured at the Bird City airport.  The thunderstorms winds blew down power lines north of Blakeman in Rawlins County, and were responsible for blowing dust at a couple locations.  The largest hail size reported was nickel near Weskan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brownout conditions reported due to the blowing dust.

During the afternoon and through the evening two groups of thunderstorms progressed across Northwest Kansas.  One group crossed into Kansas from Colorado behind a cold front, while the second group moved north across the forecast area east of the front, expanding in coverage.  The storm activity moving in from Colorado was responsible for most of the thunderstorm wind gust reports. One storm that moved across Cheyenne County Kansas produced significant straight-line wind and wind-driven hail damage. A swath of crops between 5 and 10 miles wide was completely destroyed.  Straight-line winds blew down large cotton wood trees in Sherman County.  In addition there were multiple reports of thunderstorm wind gusts above 70 MPH, with a peak gust (estimated) of 90 MPH west of St. Francis.  The largest hail reported was apple size in Oakley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Also had 0.80 of rainfall.

In addition there were multiple reports of thunderstorm wind gusts above 70 MPH, with a peak gust (estimated) of 90 MPH west of St. Francis.  The largest hail reported was apple size in Oakley.

However due to the sparce population, only one of the blowing dust pockets was confirmed.  The highest wind gust was 72 MPH measured near Weskan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak wind gust was measured near Colby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system created blizzard conditions across part of Northwest Kansas.  The blizzard conditions caused some area highways to be closed.  Up to five inches of snow was reported with the winter storm system.  South of the snow, high winds were reported in some of the counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was measured near Weskan.  Two high wind gusts were measured, one at the beginning and one at the end of time frame.

Around an three inches of snow was reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening the group of thunderstorms from East Central Colorado moved into Northwest Kansas behind a cold front.  As the thunderstorms moved into Kansas they become an organized thunderstorm complex, turning into more of a wind threat than a hail threat.  Peak wind gusts from this event were estimated at 100 MPH from damage in Hoxie and in far southern Cheyenne County.  As this complex moved east, another area of prolonged thunderstorm activity behind the cold front was occurring over Graham County where several hours of heavy rainfall had occurred.  The many hours of prolonged rainfall added up to between four and six inches of rain for the northern half of Graham County, which lead to flash flooding in Hill City.  The largest hail size reported was golf ball at two different locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unknown number of tree were blown down and a shed blew over the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms struck much of Central and South-Central Kansas with large hail and damaging winds. There were instances when & where the hail was very large, most notably on the south side of McPherson where it reached the size of baseballs. Gusts from 52 to 62 knots were measured in many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms swept east across most of the region during the afternoon and evening hours of May 2nd, 2018.  As the storms moved east, a combination of large hail and damaging winds moved across the area.  A few tornadoes were also reported along the leading edge of the quasi linear convective system with only brief touchdowns reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported the gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was the focus for late afternoon thunderstorms.

A slow moving thunderstorm developed over Wallace County and produced a wind gust of 70 MPH near Weskan.  The thunderstorm remained over Wallace County into the evening.  The prolonged heavy rainfall led to flash flooding occurring.  Many roads around Weskan had water flow over them, including Highway 40.  Over five inches of rain fell in the Weskan area.||To the north quarter size hail was reported from a thunderstorm near the Cheyenne/Sherman county line.

During the late afternoon and well into the evening, scattered strong to severe thunderstorms formed over Northwest Kansas.  The storm coverage and intensity increased during the early evening.  The strongest storms were located near an old outflow boundary.  The largest hail reported was baseball size, with one report near Herndon and two other reports near Quinter.  Thunderstorm winds broke tree limbs three to four inches in diameter northeast of Atwood.  Due to repeated rainfall over Herndon and Quinter, flash flooding occurred.  A weak tornado was reported south of Tasco near the Gove County line with a developing thunderstorm, the same storm that produced baseball size hail near Quinter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged from pea to baseball size, with the hail covering the ground.  The combination of heavy rain and hail was creating near zero visibility.

This Wednesday featured an isolated instance of quarter size hail between Palco and Zurich in Rooks County, occurring at a somewhat-unusual-for-severe-weather time of day, shortly after 10 a.m. CDT. This storm was by far the most intense one in the local six-county area, as it also dropped a fairly widespread 1-2 of rain across mainly the southern two-thirds of Rooks County before weakening as it pushed east into Osborne County. ||Weak, elevated convection first entered this portion of North Central Kansas between 8-10 a.m. CDT, expanding eastward from northwest Kansas activity that developed during the pre-dawn hours. Between 10-11 a.m. CDT, the aforementioned storm briefly reached severe levels, possibly in response to the merger of two separate/small storm clusters, one drifting east out of Graham County and another sliding northeast out of Trego/Ellis counties. Between 11 a.m. and 2 p.m. CDT, a smattering of sub-severe convection continued within the area before departing to the east. In the mid-upper levels, forcing was minimal, with North Central Kansas residing under weak quasi-zonal flow along the interface between a southern United States ridge and the main upper jet core stretched through the far northern states. At the surface, this elevated convection focused north of a west-east  frontal zone across southern Kansas. Around the time of the quarter size hail, the mesoscale environment featured mixed-layer CAPE of 1000-2000 J/kg and around 30 knots of deep-layer wind shear.

During the pre-dawn hours on this Thursday, a north-south oriented squall line/linear mesoscale convective system (MCS) tracked from west-to-east across this six-county North Central Kansas area, producing widespread winds around 50 MPH and a few measured severe-criteria gusts. Specifically, a mesonet station near Logan gusted to 62 MPH at 215 a.m. CDT, and another mesonet north of Stockton reached 67 MPH about a half hour later. There were no known ground-truth reports of storm damage. ||Digging into the details of this event a bit more, the storm complex that tracked across the local area actually resulted from the merger of two separate systems over northwest Kansas; one that had been moving southeastward from southwest Nebraska, and another that expanded northward out of southwest Kansas. Shortly after this merger, the resultant MCS entered Rooks/Phillips counties between 2-3 a.m. CDT, producing the aforementioned severe-criteria gusts. Storms exhibited a general weakening trend as they pushed across the remainder of the local area, but gusts to around 50 MPH continued until the main part of the line exited eastern Jewell/Mitchell counties around 5:30 a.m. CDT. In the mid-upper levels, this event occurred in a subtly-forced environment, as northern Kansas resided under weak westerly flow along the northern periphery of a ridge extending up from the Southern Plains. As a result, deep-layer wind shear was fairly weak, around 30 knots or less. However, late-night instability was plenty sufficient, with mixed-layer CAPE averaging 1000-2000 J/kg.

Although the majority of severe weather within the region concentrated barely to the south, east and especially north of this six-county North Central Kansas area, it was still an active Tuesday afternoon and evening of convection for the local area, featuring one severe-criteria wind report and fairly widespread heavy rain. The lone, marginally-severe report consisted of estimated 60 MPH gusts in Burr Oak at 7:30 p.m. CDT. Rainfall-wise, the vast majority of the area received at least 1, and large portions of five of the six counties (except Phillips) received at least 2-3 of rather-needed rain. However, somewhat excessive amounts of 3-4+ occurred in much of Mitchell, Smith and eastern Jewell counties, with some of the highest official 24-hour totals (NWS coop and CoCoRaHS observers) featuring: 4.63 at Formoso, 4.33 near Webber, 4.30 at Smith Center and 3.87 at Cawker City. Although there were surely instances of fairly minor flooding and possibly even brief flash flooding, ground truth reports were rather limited. However, one automated river gauge in the area detected minor flooding, as the gauge along the White Rock Creek east of Burr Oak eventually crested around 16.6 ft. on the morning of the 21st (flood stage is 15 ft.). ||Breaking down event evolution/timing, isolated strong storms bubbled up intermittently over mainly Mitchell/Jewell counties between 3:30-6:00 p.m. CDT. Then during the ensuing three hours, convection increased in coverage and intensity across the area as a whole, due in part to one storm complex building southward out of Nebraska, and another brushing into the area out of west-central Kansas. By around 9 p.m. CDT, the strongest storms had departed to the east-southeast. However, between 10 p.m. and 1 a.m. CDT, the southern fringes of the local area were skirted by the northern edges of a squall line tracking through central Kansas, bringing another uptick in gusty sub-severe winds and heavy rain before all activity finally faded for the night. Closing with some meteorological background, this day held well-anticipated potential for severe storms and heavy rain, as low-amplitude shortwave energy tracked into Kansas around the southeast periphery of a larger-scale trough/closed low centered over the Northern Rockies. At the surface, a fairly well-defined and generally west-east oriented quasi-stationary front stretched near the Nebraska-Kansas-border, right across the heart of the local area. This promoted an environment characterized by east-southeast breezes, afternoon temperatures in the upper 80s-low 90s F and dewpoints in the mid 60s-low 70s F. During the height of evening convection, the mesoscale environment featured between 30-35 knots of deep-layer wind shear and mixed-layer CAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 60 MPH.

However, one automated river gauge in the area detected minor flooding, as the gauge along the White Rock Creek east of Burr Oak eventually crested around 16.6 ft. on the morning of the 21st (flood stage is 15 ft.). ||Breaking down event evolution/timing, isolated strong storms bubbled up intermittently over mainly Mitchell/Jewell counties between 3:30-6:00 p.m. CDT. Then during the ensuing three hours, convection increased in coverage and intensity across the area as a whole, due in part to one storm complex building southward out of Nebraska, and another brushing into the area out of west-central Kansas. By around 9 p.m. CDT, the strongest storms had departed to the east-southeast. However, between 10 p.m. and 1 a.m. CDT, the southern fringes of the local area were skirted by the northern edges of a squall line tracking through central Kansas, bringing another uptick in gusty sub-severe winds and heavy rain before all activity finally faded for the night. Closing with some meteorological background, this day held well-anticipated potential for severe storms and heavy rain, as low-amplitude shortwave energy tracked into Kansas around the southeast periphery of a larger-scale trough/closed low centered over the Northern Rockies. At the surface, a fairly well-defined and generally west-east oriented quasi-stationary front stretched near the Nebraska-Kansas-border, right across the heart of the local area. This promoted an environment characterized by east-southeast breezes, afternoon temperatures in the upper 80s-low 90s F and dewpoints in the mid 60s-low 70s F. During the height of evening convection, the mesoscale environment featured between 30-35 knots of deep-layer wind shear and mixed-layer CAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total rainfall amount of 3.87 inches was recorded at Cawker City, with 3.56 inches recorded 10 miles south-southwest of Beloit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and well into the evening, scattered strong to severe thunderstorms formed over Northwest Kansas.  The storm coverage and intensity increased during the early evening.  The strongest storms were located near an old outflow boundary.  The largest hail reported was baseball size, with one report near Herndon and two other reports near Quinter.  Thunderstorm winds broke tree limbs three to four inches in diameter northeast of Atwood.  Due to repeated rainfall over Herndon and Quinter, flash flooding occurred.  A weak tornado was reported south of Tasco near the Gove County line with a developing thunderstorm, the same storm that produced baseball size hail near Quinter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early in the afternoon scattered thunderstorms developed north of a warm front.  Penny size hail was reported from one of these storms.  During the latter half of the afternoon a thunderstorm complex moved east out of Colorado into Northwest Kansas.  The complex continued to increase in coverage as it moved eastward, transitioning into more of a wind threat than a hail threat.  Winds from the thunderstorm complex blew trees down, some of them being large trees, blew power lines down, and damaged a grain bin.  There were very few hail reports with this thunderstorm complex, with the largest being only penny size from several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Unknown number of trees blown down in town.

During the late afternoon and well into the evening, scattered strong to severe thunderstorms formed over Northwest Kansas.  The storm coverage and intensity increased during the early evening.  The strongest storms were located near an old outflow boundary.  The largest hail reported was baseball size, with one report near Herndon and two other reports near Quinter.  Thunderstorm winds broke tree limbs three to four inches in diameter northeast of Atwood.  Due to repeated rainfall over Herndon and Quinter, flash flooding occurred.  A weak tornado was reported south of Tasco near the Gove County line with a developing thunderstorm, the same storm that produced baseball size hail near Quinter.

During the evening the group of thunderstorms from East Central Colorado moved into Northwest Kansas behind a cold front.  As the thunderstorms moved into Kansas they become an organized thunderstorm complex, turning into more of a wind threat than a hail threat.  Peak wind gusts from this event were estimated at 100 MPH from damage in Hoxie and in far southern Cheyenne County.  As this complex moved east, another area of prolonged thunderstorm activity behind the cold front was occurring over Graham County where several hours of heavy rainfall had occurred.  The many hours of prolonged rainfall added up to between four and six inches of rain for the northern half of Graham County, which lead to flash flooding in Hill City.  The largest hail size reported was golf ball at two different locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged from dime to golf ball in size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pre-dawn hours on this Saturday featured a very isolated instance of wind damage in the Beloit area, as estimated 70 MPH winds around 4:45 a.m. CDT peeled the roof off a floral business on the south side of town and also downed a few power lines. Rather oddly, there were no other reports of storm damage in the area, and the nearby airport recorded a peak gust of only 44 MPH, serving as further evidence to the quite-localized nature of these damaging winds. ||Despite the lack of obvious severe weather signatures via radar or automated surface observations, there were a few seemingly-weak thunderstorms in the area, which might have interacted in some manner with a southward-moving outflow boundary to promote damaging winds. This outflow boundary was associated with what had earlier been a severe-wind producing linear mesoscale convective system (MCS) in south central/southeast Nebraska, which had since weakened after pushing southward into Kansas. Many hours before reaching the local area, the initial severe convection that spawned this outflow boundary developed over south central South Dakota during the late afternoon/early evening of the 8th before surging south-southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very isolated and focused wind damage occurred. A roof was peeled off a business on the south side of Beloit, and there were also a few power lines downed. There were no other reports of wind damage in the area, and the nearby airport recorded a peak wind gust of only 44 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and through the evening two groups of thunderstorms progressed across Northwest Kansas.  One group crossed into Kansas from Colorado behind a cold front, while the second group moved north across the forecast area east of the front, expanding in coverage.  The storm activity moving in from Colorado was responsible for most of the thunderstorm wind gust reports. One storm that moved across Cheyenne County Kansas produced significant straight-line wind and wind-driven hail damage. A swath of crops between 5 and 10 miles wide was completely destroyed.  Straight-line winds blew down large cotton wood trees in Sherman County.  In addition there were multiple reports of thunderstorm wind gusts above 70 MPH, with a peak gust (estimated) of 90 MPH west of St. Francis.  The largest hail reported was apple size in Oakley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and through the evening a group of thunderstorms moved in from the northwest behind a cold front.  Storm activity increased in coverage as the storms moved southeast, transitioning from producing mainly hail in Eastern Colorado to straight-line winds and flash flooding.  The straight-line winds blew down power lines in western Sherman County and blew down or damaged multiple trees at the travel center on I-70.  Multiple trees were uprooted in Tribune from the straight-line winds as well.  The largest hail size reported was quarter size south of Ruleton.  This line of storms also produced torrential rainfall over the western part of Sherman County, causing some county roads to be closed.||Meanwhile to the west a second group of thunderstorms had formed behind the cold front.  The strength of these storms was less, however thunderstorm winds caused major damage to a barn northwest of Norcatur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 70 MPH were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm producing large hail and damaging winds moved south across far Northwest Kansas behind a weak surface boundary.  The largest hail reported was golf ball size near Goodland.  The strongest wind gust reported was 60 MPH behind the storm in northeast Sherman County.  The combination of hail and winds from the thunderstorm broke house windows near Goodland.  At one point the storm produced hail of an unknown size to a depth of one to two inches on Highway 27 in far southern Cheyenne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occurred with estimated wind gusts up to 70 MPH.

While much of the area saw some relief from the drought, many counties remained in D2-D4 status through the month of September. While the full scope of drought impacts are unknown, many farmers took losses on their hay and corn, opting to bale it for livestock or knock it down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought of 2018 continued for Johnson County, however despite an influx of some moisture only negligible relief came to the county. Conditions held at D4 during the month of September, but the impacts and losses of several crops were already felt across the region. The amount of damages is unknown at this point, but numerous farmers were unable to get full return from their crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere became very unstable after temperatures rose to around 100 during the afternoon with mid to upper 60 dep points.  A weak frontal boundary or remnants of an old outflow boundary provided focus for enough convergence to initiate thunderstorms.

The largest severe weather outbreak of the year began in the morning.  The storms activity spread west across Northwest Kansas, eventually expanding into Southwest Nebraska and East Central Colorado.  The first tornado of the day for Northwest Kansas occurred near Hill City.  No damage was reported from the tornado.  The largest hail size reported was golf ball size from multiple locations area.  Some of the thunderstorms did produce damaging wind gusts, which blew down power lines, large trees, and damaged farm buildings.  Flash Flooding occurred over Graham and Gove counties where prolonged moderate to heavy rain that began in the morning, lasted into the late evening.  The flash flooding caused some culverts along Highway 24 to be washed out.  The flooding was so significant water rescues were performed at a trailer park on the south side of Hill City, and 20 cattle drowned east of Bogue.  By the end of the day parts of Graham County had up to a foot of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and through the evening a group of thunderstorms moved in from the northwest behind a cold front.  Storm activity increased in coverage as the storms moved southeast, transitioning from producing mainly hail in Eastern Colorado to straight-line winds and flash flooding.  The straight-line winds blew down power lines in western Sherman County and blew down or damaged multiple trees at the travel center on I-70.  Multiple trees were uprooted in Tribune from the straight-line winds as well.  The largest hail size reported was quarter size south of Ruleton.  This line of storms also produced torrential rainfall over the western part of Sherman County, causing some county roads to be closed.||Meanwhile to the west a second group of thunderstorms had formed behind the cold front.  The strength of these storms was less, however thunderstorm winds caused major damage to a barn northwest of Norcatur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged from pea to quarter in size, with hail piling up in the yard.

While much of the area saw some relief from the drought, many counties remained in D2-D4 status through the month of September. While the full scope of drought impacts are unknown, many farmers took losses on their hay and corn, opting to bale it for livestock or knock it down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought of 2018 continued for Atchison County, however despite an influx of some moisture only negligible relief came to the county. Conditions held at D3 during the month of September, but the impacts and losses of several crops were already felt across the region. The amount of damages is unknown at this point, but numerous farmers were unable to get full return from their crops.

During the late afternoon and well into the evening, scattered strong to severe thunderstorms formed over Northwest Kansas.  The storm coverage and intensity increased during the early evening.  The strongest storms were located near an old outflow boundary.  The largest hail reported was baseball size, with one report near Herndon and two other reports near Quinter.  Thunderstorm winds broke tree limbs three to four inches in diameter northeast of Atwood.  Due to repeated rainfall over Herndon and Quinter, flash flooding occurred.  A weak tornado was reported south of Tasco near the Gove County line with a developing thunderstorm, the same storm that produced baseball size hail near Quinter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged from nickel to golf ball then to quarter in size.  The hail was still falling at the end of this time frame.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The largest severe weather outbreak of the year began in the morning.  The storms activity spread west across Northwest Kansas, eventually expanding into Southwest Nebraska and East Central Colorado.  The first tornado of the day for Northwest Kansas occurred near Hill City.  No damage was reported from the tornado.  The largest hail size reported was golf ball size from multiple locations area.  Some of the thunderstorms did produce damaging wind gusts, which blew down power lines, large trees, and damaged farm buildings.  Flash Flooding occurred over Graham and Gove counties where prolonged moderate to heavy rain that began in the morning, lasted into the late evening.  The flash flooding caused some culverts along Highway 24 to be washed out.  The flooding was so significant water rescues were performed at a trailer park on the south side of Hill City, and 20 cattle drowned east of Bogue.  By the end of the day parts of Graham County had up to a foot of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was measured by a storm chaser.

Precipitation picked up during August, especially in some of the hardest hit drought areas, but in a lot of cases the damage had already been done, and while the rains did pick back up the ground soil was so parched that it made hardly a dent in the drought across northeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While rain did pick up across the area through the month of August, drought conditions saw some improvement by the end of the month. The impacts and economic impact across far eastern Kansas as a result of the drought are as of yet unclear, but across much of the Midwest several crops and fields were deemed unfit for harvest and were either lost or baled for cattle feed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe single cell and multicell storms affected portions of north central Kansas around midday on this Thursday. Isolated thunderstorms began forming around 930 AM CST. Other storms formed and by 1030 AM CST, a northwest-southeast oriented broken line of thunderstorms had evolved from Phillips to Osborne counties. From 1030 AM until 1230 PM CST, the line morphed more into a cluster of cells which eventually formed a common cold pool. By 1200 PM CST, a short squall line had evolved with the northern portion of the line over Jewell and Mitchell counties. These storms produced localized downburst winds measured between 50 and 66 mph, and possibly as high as 75 mph based on a spotters estimation. No damage was reported. A few spots also received nickel to quarter size hail.||The storms formed along the weak cold front. During the previous night, weak cyclogenesis occurred over South Dakota, with warm frontogenesis occurring to its southeast, and cold frontogenesis occurring to its southwest over Nebraska. As the South Dakota low moved into Minnesota around midday, the cold front continue moving southeast, crossing north central Kansas. In the upper-levels, the flow was characterized by low-amplitude west-northwest flow. A subtropical high was over the Desert Southwest with a deepening shortwave trough crossing the Northern Plains into the Upper Midwest. Just prior to convection initiation, temperatures had climbed into the middle 80s in the presence of dewpoints that were in the lower 70s. Combined with steep mid-level lapse rates of 7-7.5 C/km, this resulted in MLCAPE between 1500 and 2500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was around 45 kt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon and evening of August 16 several thunderstorms formed across eastern Kansas. These storms produced some large hail between 1 to 1.5 inch.

An upper level trough with embedded minor disturbances approached western Kansas late in the day.  Combined with daytime heating, this caused vigorous convection to develop by early evening, which lingered through the night. These storms caused numerous flooding issues across western Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.87 inches was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low-end severe hail occurred over western Rooks county late on this Wednesday afternoon. A couple isolated thunderstorms formed early in the afternoon over south central Nebraska. By 3 PM CST, the storms near McCook began crossing into north central Kansas. By 4 PM, a strong storm had developed over Norton county. That storm became severe as it moved southeast through eastern Graham county. The storm remained primarily over Graham county, but its core was close enough to the Rooks county line that quarter size hail fell in the town of Damar.||The storm and the others nearby formed between a stationary front across northern Kansas and an approaching cold front dropping south through Nebraska. The upper-levels were characterized by west-northwest flow. A subtropical high was over the Desert Southwest with a ridge up to Oregon and Washington, and a trough over the Appalachians. An upper low was over Ontario with a shortwave trough progressing from the Northern Plains into the Upper Midwest. Temperatures ahead of the storm were in the upper 80s, with dewpoints in the middle 60s. Combined with mid-level lapse rates around 7 C/km, this resulted in MLCAPE near 2000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was around 50 kt.

A slow moving thunderstorm developed over Wallace County and produced a wind gust of 70 MPH near Weskan.  The thunderstorm remained over Wallace County into the evening.  The prolonged heavy rainfall led to flash flooding occurring.  Many roads around Weskan had water flow over them, including Highway 40.  Over five inches of rain fell in the Weskan area.||To the north quarter size hail was reported from a thunderstorm near the Cheyenne/Sherman county line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many of the roads around Weskan had water running over them in low lying areas.  Highway 40 had water running over it in a few places as well.  Over five inches of rainfall was measured in Weskan before the rain gauge overflowed.  No reports of flood damage to roads were given.

The storms activity spread west across Northwest Kansas, eventually expanding into Southwest Nebraska and East Central Colorado.  The first tornado of the day for Northwest Kansas occurred near Hill City.  No damage was reported from the tornado.  The largest hail size reported was golf ball size from multiple locations area.  Some of the thunderstorms did produce damaging wind gusts, which blew down power lines, large trees, and damaged farm buildings.  Flash Flooding occurred over Graham and Gove counties where prolonged moderate to heavy rain that began in the morning, lasted into the late evening.  The flash flooding caused some culverts along Highway 24 to be washed out.  The flooding was so significant water rescues were performed at a trailer park on the south side of Hill City, and 20 cattle drowned east of Bogue.  By the end of the day parts of Graham County had up to a foot of rainfall.

During the late afternoon thunderstorms developed behind a southward moving cold front.  Hail up to golf ball size was reported near Nicodemus and in Bogue.  Winds from the thunderstorms were strong enough to blow down a tree in Bogue and snap two power poles south of Bogue.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two power poles were broken off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the afternoon thunderstorms developed near a stalled front extending northeast across Northwest Kansas, with storm activity lasting well into the evening.  The highest thunderstorm wind gust was estimated at 70 MPH near Norton.  The largest hail size reported was half dollar near Winona.  Dust storm conditions were reported near Oakley from thunderstorm winds that lifted dust into the air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds shook the house and knocked a very heavy double trash dumpster on its side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the latter half of the afternoon strong to severe thunderstorms moved north across Northwest Kansas.  Storm coverage increased in the late afternoon along an outflow boundary from one of storms as it moved north.  Behind the outflow boundary wind gusts around 60 MPH were reported, which caused some damage.   The largest hail reported was golf ball size near Colby.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to ping-pong ball size hail was reported with this storm.

The largest severe weather outbreak of the year began in the morning.  The storms activity spread west across Northwest Kansas, eventually expanding into Southwest Nebraska and East Central Colorado.  The first tornado of the day for Northwest Kansas occurred near Hill City.  No damage was reported from the tornado.  The largest hail size reported was golf ball size from multiple locations area.  Some of the thunderstorms did produce damaging wind gusts, which blew down power lines, large trees, and damaged farm buildings.  Flash Flooding occurred over Graham and Gove counties where prolonged moderate to heavy rain that began in the morning, lasted into the late evening.  The flash flooding caused some culverts along Highway 24 to be washed out.  The flooding was so significant water rescues were performed at a trailer park on the south side of Hill City, and 20 cattle drowned east of Bogue.  By the end of the day parts of Graham County had up to a foot of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail measured at MM 107 on I-70.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and well into the evening, scattered strong to severe thunderstorms formed over Northwest Kansas.  The storm coverage and intensity increased during the early evening.  The strongest storms were located near an old outflow boundary.  The largest hail reported was baseball size, with one report near Herndon and two other reports near Quinter.  Thunderstorm winds broke tree limbs three to four inches in diameter northeast of Atwood.  Due to repeated rainfall over Herndon and Quinter, flash flooding occurred.  A weak tornado was reported south of Tasco near the Gove County line with a developing thunderstorm, the same storm that produced baseball size hail near Quinter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A number of streets are underwater in Quinter.  The bottom of the ramp at exit 107 has water roughly eight to ten inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the afternoon thunderstorms developed near a stalled front extending northeast across Northwest Kansas, with storm activity lasting well into the evening.  The highest thunderstorm wind gust was estimated at 70 MPH near Norton.  The largest hail size reported was half dollar near Winona.  Dust storm conditions were reported near Oakley from thunderstorm winds that lifted dust into the air.
